nativeRange.habitat: Sandy and muddy bottoms at depths of approximately 29–230 m; demersal marine environment

diet.notes: Has been documented eating small crustaceans, polychaete worms, small fish, clams, and zoobenthos in the wild. In aquaria, generally advised to start with live saltwater feeder shrimp to entice feeding; may eventually accept frozen shrimp, krill, and similar meaty foods with target feeding. Has been known to refuse food when first introduced.

avoidWith: small invertebrates such as shrimp and crabs

summary: The Longnose Batfish (Ogcocephalus corniger) is an unusual bottom-dwelling species from the Western Atlantic that walks along sandy seafloors using modified fins rather than swimming. It is generally considered challenging to keep, primarily due to its specialized feeding requirements.
careNotes:
The Longnose Batfish is a highly distinctive, dorsoventrally flattened (strongly compressed from top to bottom) fish that tends to walk or crawl along the sea floor using its modified pectoral and pelvic fins rather than swim. It carries a characteristic elongated rostral projection — a moveable, antenna-like lure on the tip of its snout — which it uses to attract prey within striking distance. In an aquarium, it generally requires a sand or mud substrate, dim lighting, and low water flow to feel settled.

Feeding is commonly the greatest challenge with this species. When first introduced, live saltwater feeder shrimp are generally advised to initiate a feeding response. Target feeding — delivering food directly to the fish using a feeding stick or turkey baster — is often recommended to ensure the batfish receives enough food without having to compete with faster tankmates. Some individuals have been known to transition to frozen meaty foods such as shrimp and krill over time, while others may continue to require live prey.

The Longnose Batfish tends to be peaceful toward fish and invertebrates too large to swallow, but it is an ambush predator that will attempt to consume smaller fish and any shrimp or crabs within reach. Fast-moving or aggressive feeding tankmates will routinely outcompete it at mealtimes, so tankmate selection requires care.

A minimum tank of around 400 litres is recommended to provide sufficient open sandy bottom area for a fish with this behavioral profile. A tightly fitted lid is important — the species has been observed jumping and crawling out of the water, and its escape risk is considered high.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has not been documented. Very limited information is available on reproductive behavior.

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy, native range (Cuba, Gulf of Mexico, Bahamas, USA), adult size (23 cm), and temperature (25–28°C from FishBase data). No confirmed captive keeping records reported.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding documented. Very limited information available on reproductive behavior in the wild or aquarium.
